all_hints_text:
it raises ValueError exception (not IndexError) in Python3.9.
By default, in an effort to maintain runtime performance, indexed lookups in Transcrypt default to using native JS lookups that only return `undefined` for an invalid index. This is the reason that [enabling operator overloading for processing negative indices](https://www.transcrypt.org/docs/html/differences_cpython.html?highlight=negative%20indices#differences-due-to-the-lean-and-mean-design-goal) is required, as it will instead call a custom `__getitem__` method for handling indexed lookups. As a compromise, I've added index validation to that same `__getitem__` method that is used when operator overloading is enabled with the `opov` pragma. In that case, the following would result in an exception that can be caught:

```python
lst = ['a', 'b', 'c']
try:
    idx_val = lst[4]  # __:opov
    print(idx_val)
except Exception as e:
    print(f"{type(e).__name__}: {e}")

# IndexError: index out of range
```
Autotests are all passing with this change, though I am concerned with potential backwards compatibility issues that some may have. That said, enabling this behavior with `opov` moves it closer to that of CPython even if it is a bit less performant. 

This feature will be added in v3.9.4



Thanks for the fix.
I think this should be in the next major version.
Still want to take a good long look at it.
And indeed at least there should be a decent error report.
Will be fixed in v3.9.4
This fix (currently on [dev_3.9.4 branch](https://github.com/TranscryptOrg/Transcrypt/tree/dev_3.9.4)) required a bit more extra logic to cover edge cases, but I feel slicing is a foundational feature of Python and the extra code in the runtime is worth being able to move the behavior closer to that of CPython. I also added validations for invalid slices similar to CPython. The updated code shouldn't slow down slicing much for the more common step==1 use cases.

Indeed.
Will be added in v3.9.4

Will be added, thanks!
Will be added in v3.9.4
Hi! At our company, after transcrypting the code, we bundle javascript using Closure Compiler.
This new `splitlines` function causes this error:
```
ERROR - [JSC_UNTRANSPILABLE] Cannot convert ECMASCRIPT_2018 feature "RegExp Lookbehind" to targeted output language.
```

The problem is on `str.split(/(?<=\n)(?=\n)|(?<=[\r\n])(?=[^\r\n])/g)`.

Solution could be just reverting to `transcrypt==3.9.3`.

But I leave here a patch for `org/transcrypt/__builtin__.js`, just in case you want to consider to modify it for future versions (see attached `transcrypt-3.9.4.patch`).

Also attached you can find `transcrypt-3.9.4-splitlines.js`, which runs some tests over both versions of `splitlines()`, getting consistent results.

I hope it helps. Thanks for your work!

*The patch*

[transcrypt-3.9.4.patch](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25370672/transcrypt-3.9.4.patch)

*The tests*

[transcrypt-3.9.4-splitlines.js](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/25370678/transcrypt-3.9.4-splitlines.js)


Will be fixed in v3.9.4

Will be added in v3.9.4

I know this was opened a long time ago, but thank you @phfaist for providing a clean example and fix.
I also found an issue with `dict.popitem()` popping from the front of the list instead of the back like CPython and fixed that while I was in there as well.

Will be added in v3.9.4
